focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Melanie's hard block numbers sat in Carmen's head all through the next day.

That night, Carmen waited until Devon left the station.

He did this every night around twelve-thirty — walked to the kitchen, ate whatever the dietary aide had left out, came back in twenty minutes smelling like butterscotch pudding. She'd never had a reason to track his schedule before. Tonight she had timed it on her watch, standing at the sub-station pretending to chart, marking the moment he pulled out his earbuds and shuffled toward the kitchen in his untied Nikes. Twelve thirty-one. She gave him two minutes' head start.

The hallway was dim at this hour, half the fluorescents off, the others buzzing at a frequency she could feel at the base of her skull. Wing A stretched ahead of her — a single corridor of closed doors and the faint ammonia smell that never fully left the linoleum, no matter how many times the overnight aide mopped. The AC had been struggling all week. Early September in central New Jersey and Lakeview Gardens was still holding heat like a kiln, the building's cinder-block walls radiating warmth back into the hallways hours after dark. She could feel it through the soles of her sneakers.

She carried her clipboard. She didn't need it — she wasn't charting, wasn't rounding, wasn't doing any of the things a clipboard authorized her to do at twelve-thirty in the morning. But the clipboard was habit, the same way the badge clipped to her scrub top was habit. The tools of a nurse who belonged in this hallway at this hour. Cover.

She passed Room 110 and heard Mrs. Delacroix making her sounds — a low continuous murmur that rose and fell with her breathing, each exhale catching just enough in her throat to turn air into voice. Carmen had checked on her at eleven. S...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...is and paid its night staff fourteen-fifty an hour to sleep through it.

Harry stopped at his door — 114A, the single room, a privilege earned by seniority and the administrative assumption that he needed quiet for his condition. He wheeled in. He transferred to the bed, the movement efficient even though no one was watching and the performance was for empty walls. He pulled the thin blanket to his chest. The window unit rattled. The room was warm and close, the air thick with late August and the stale air of a space one person lived in and never left.

Carmen Reyes sat in his head like a number that refused to reconcile. A report would have brought supervisors, forms, a round of disbelief, the confusion defense. Carmen would work quieter than that. She could start recounting on her own, stay through med pass and watch Lola swallow every pill, ask why Burt came up short on oxycodone before his refill date, close one small gap after another until the extra tablets vanished from the margins.

Harry opened his eyes. By the time anyone upstairs understood what she was looking at, the cards could be clean and the cash already gone.

Outside his window, the parking lot was empty except for Devon's Civic and the overnight nurse's minivan. The streetlight at the lot's edge cast a yellow circle on the asphalt. He had just under an hour before Devon's alarm and less than that before Carmen started turning the night into paperwork.

Waiting had sounded reasonable at the table. In bed it looked like the sort of arithmetic people defended right up until the audit.

Harry sat up. The mattress springs complained. Three clean days required cleaner numbers than the ones they'd left on the books.

He pulled on the cardigan, took the bath towel from the chair, and went back into the hall.
